one_line_takeaway: GAVEL reframes LLM activation-based safety monitoring as a cybersecurity-style rule engine over composable, interpretable 'Cognitive Elements', achieving near-zero false positives (avg FPR 0.00 vs 0.02-0.60 for baselines) and AUC ~0.99 across 9 misuse categories on Mistral-7B while remaining robust to text-evasion attacks that collapse a GPT-4 judge baseline's detection rate.
key_finding: On Mistral-7B, GAVEL (paper's Activation Classifier row, GAVEL) attains the best precision/recall balance across all 9 misuse categories: average AUC 0.99, average balanced accuracy 0.96, and average FPR 0.00 (Table 3), with per-category FPR at or near 0.00 for 8/9 categories (0.02 for Elections and Tax Authority). Comparators in the same table: CircuitBreakers (avg AUC 0.68, avg b-ACC 0.69, avg FPR 0.06), RepBending fine-tuning (avg AUC 0.87, avg b-ACC 0.87, avg FPR 0.02), CAST (avg AUC 0.68, avg b-ACC 0.59, avg FPR 0.60), JBShield (avg AUC 0.41, avg b-ACC 0.63, avg FPR 0.01), Llama Guard 4 (avg AUC 0.87, avg b-ACC 0.93, avg FPR 0.03), Google Perspective API (avg AUC 0.53, avg b-ACC 0.55, avg FPR 0.02), OpenAI Moderator API (avg AUC 0.69, avg b-ACC 0.69, avg FPR 0.00), and a coarse per-misuse-category 'Activation Classifier' baseline (avg AUC 0.97, avg b-ACC 0.92, avg FPR 0.07). On deployment-style benign background traffic, GAVEL with Mistral-7B achieved 0.088 FPR on UltraChat and 0.008 FPR on DialogueSum (Table 8). Under adversarial evasion of a GPT-4 judge LLM baseline, GAVEL was reported to maintain >85% recall across all categories while GPT-4's detection collapsed under Misdirection attacks (e.g., to 12% on Delusional Thinking and 15% on Election Interference). On a separate Deceptive Marketing case study (self-promotion woven into medical advice), GPT-4 detected only 24% of cases versus 73% for GAVEL (via co-occurrence of the Build Trust and Healthcare/Taxation-adjacent CEs). Automated CE/rule generation applied zero-shot to three external benchmarks (PKU-SafeRLHF phishing, ReasoningShield political risk, ToxiGen hate speech) achieved 0.97 TPR on Political Risk and average 0.945 TPR on ToxiGen without manual tuning.
core_contribution: The paper introduces GAVEL (Governance via Activation-based Verification and Extensible Logic), a rule-based activation-monitoring framework for LLM safety. Its central novelty is 'Cognitive Elements' (CEs): fine-grained, interpretable, token-level primitives of model behavior (e.g., 'making a threat', 'payment tools', 'masquerade as human') extracted from internal activations, which can be composed with Boolean logic (AND/OR/NOT) into human-readable rules (e.g., 'refuse if task:creating content AND (directive:click OR directive:grant OR directive:personal information)') that fire on rule violations, explicitly modeled after signature-sharing conventions in cybersecurity tools like Snort, YARA, and OSSEC. The authors define 23 CEs (Table 1) organized into Directive-to-User, LLM Task, and Topic categories, and 9 misuse-category rule predicates (Table 2) spanning cybercrime (phishing, SQL injection), psychological harm (delusion reinforcement, anti-LGBTQ content, election propaganda, racism), and scam automation (tax-authority impersonation, romance baiting, e-commerce scams). They also release an open-source agentic tool that automatically generates CEs, rule sets, and excitation datasets from a natural-language description of a target policy/violation, plus a GUI ('GAVEL Studio') for rule authoring and test-time CE visualization. Code and datasets are released at github.com/Offensive-AI-Lab/gavel.
threat_model: The paper targets LLM misuse where harmful intent is present in the model's internal computation but obscured or paraphrased at the surface-text level ('representation attacks' bypassing text-only or output-based safeguards). Their own evaluation explicitly stress-tests three adversarial strategies against text-only moderation and GAVEL: Misdirection (diluting harmful content with benign chatter), Goal Hijacking (prompt injection attempting to override the LLM monitor's instructions), and Evasion (having an LLM rephrase the conversation to be stealthier). They assume white-box/gray-box access to the target model's internal activations (attention outputs at chosen layers), i.e., the defender is the model owner deploying an on-device monitor, not an external adversary.
limitations: The paper's own 'Discussion on Limitations' section acknowledges: (1) the Boolean/predicate rule formalism may seem too restrictive to capture the full richness of abstract neural concepts and behaviors -- the authors argue this rigidity is intentional (a strength for auditability) rather than resolving the expressiveness concern; (2) rule and CE authoring is inherently labor-intensive without their automation tooling, and scalability depends on the agent-driven CE/rule generation pipeline and community sharing actually materializing; (3) the framework explicitly does not evaluate enforcement actions beyond alert/stop (refusal and steering mitigations are stated as reusable from prior work but not tested here); (4) the evaluation datasets (misuse and benign) are themselves synthetically generated by GPT-4.1 and validated by GPT-5 rather than sourced from real deployment logs, which could bias detection difficulty and CE separability; (5) results are demonstrated on a curated set of 9 misuse categories chosen by the authors rather than an exhaustive or community-standardized benchmark, which the authors state is deliberate ('we do not attempt to exhaustively develop rules for existing benchmark datasets... best pursued as a community effort').

datasets_used: 14,950 GPT-4.1-generated, GPT-5-validated multi-turn misuse and benign conversations across 9 misuse categories (150 misuse + 500 benign per category)
UltraChat (Ding et al., 2023) and DialogueSum (Chen et al., 2021) — 1,000-conversation benign background set for deployment FPR measurement
PKU-SafeRLHF (Ji et al., 2024) — phishing guidance benchmark, used for automated CE-generation transfer test
ReasoningShield (Li et al., 2025) — political risk benchmark, used for automated CE-generation transfer test
ToxiGen (Hartvigsen et al., 2022) — hate speech benchmark, used for automated CE-generation transfer test
quantitative_results: GAVEL on Mistral-7B: avg AUC 0.99, avg balanced accuracy 0.96, avg FPR 0.00 across 9 misuse categories (Table 3)
GAVEL avg TPR 95.5% using attention-layer outputs vs 82.3% using MLP outputs (layer-choice ablation)
GAVEL FPR on benign background traffic: 0.088 (UltraChat), 0.008 (DialogueSum) (Table 8)

GAVEL recall >85% across all categories under adversarial Misdirection/Goal Hijacking/Evasion attacks, vs GPT-4 judge dropping to 12% (Delusional Thinking) and 15% (Election Interference) under Misdirection
Deceptive Marketing case study: GAVEL 73% detection vs GPT-4 judge 24% detection
Automated CE-generation transfer: 0.97 TPR on ReasoningShield Political Risk, average 0.945 TPR on ToxiGen
54% of detected malicious dialogues involved tokens with multiple simultaneously active CEs (co-occurrence rate)
baselines_compared: CircuitBreakers (Zou et al., 2024) — avg AUC 0.68, avg b-ACC 0.69, avg FPR 0.06
RepBending fine-tuning (Yousefpour et al., 2025) — avg AUC 0.87, avg b-ACC 0.87, avg FPR 0.02
CAST (Lee et al., 2024) — avg AUC 0.68, avg b-ACC 0.59, avg FPR 0.60
JBShield (Zhang et al., 2025) — avg AUC 0.41, avg b-ACC 0.63, avg FPR 0.01
Llama Guard 4 (Inan et al., 2023) — avg AUC 0.87, avg b-ACC 0.93, avg FPR 0.03
Google Perspective API (Lees et al., 2022) — avg AUC 0.53, avg b-ACC 0.55, avg FPR 0.02
OpenAI Moderator API (OpenAI, 2023) — avg AUC 0.69, avg b-ACC 0.69, avg FPR 0.00
Coarse per-misuse-category Activation Classifier (non-CE granularity) — avg AUC 0.97, avg b-ACC 0.92, avg FPR 0.07
GPT-4 judge LLM (text-only, explicitly prompted per category) — matched GAVEL on plain-text violations but collapsed under adversarial evasion strategies
